title: Spinning from a cloud batt
subtitle: How to prepare and spin a lofty, airy drum-carded batt for a soft, woollen-style yarn
excerpt: A cloud batt is a very lightly carded, airy preparation that produces a soft, lofty woollen-style yarn with good insulating properties. This entry covers how to prepare, pre-draft, and spin from a cloud batt to produce a consistent singles.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional drum-carding and spinning practice. Cloud batts are documented in British and American spinning guild teaching as a beginner-friendly commercial preparation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A very lightly drum-carded fibre preparation that is airy and low-density compared with a standard batt. The fibres are loosely aligned and the batt pulls apart easily into a spinning-ready roving.
Drawing out a thick batt or roving into a thinner, more even preparation before spinning. Pre-drafting a cloud batt reduces the amount of work the wheel or spindle has to do during spinning.
A yarn spun from carded, randomly-aligned fibres using a long draw technique. More lofty and airy than worsted yarn; good for warm, soft garments.

content.text: is one of the most forgiving starting points for beginners.
the batt before sitting at the wheel does most of the work, and the result is a soft,
with good loft. The main challenge is keeping the pre-drafted strip even in thickness before it reaches the wheel.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. Tie hair back before sitting at the wheel.

items.name: Cloud batt in any fibre (merino cloud batts are the most commonly available)

content.text: Step 1: Tear the batt into strips

Take the cloud batt and gently tear it into lengthways strips about 5 cm wide. A cloud batt tears very easily because it is lightly carded; no tools are needed. Stack the strips loosely in a basket beside the wheel.

Take one strip and draw it out lengthways by gently pulling the two ends apart. This pre-drafts the strip from about 5 cm wide into a narrower, more even ribbon about 2 to 3 cm across. The ribbon should be thin enough to spin easily but not so thin that it tears. Pre-draft a few strips before beginning to spin so you have a ready supply.
Step 3: Spin with a long draw'

Attach the pre-drafted strip to the leader and treadle steadily at a moderate ratio (7:1 to 9:1). Feed the fibres in with a long draw: hold the supply of fibre in one hand and draw it back with the other, allowing the twist to run into the drafting zone from a distance of 20 to 30 cm. The cloud batt fibres are randomised and respond well to this open approach.

When one strip is almost gone, overlap the beginning of the next strip with the tail of the previous by 3 to 4 cm and spin through the join. If the join feels lumpy, split the joining ends thinner before overlapping.

items.problem: The pre-drafted strip tears instead of drawing out
items.cause: The strip is being pulled from a single point rather than drawn evenly from both ends
items.fix: Hold the strip at two points about 15 cm apart and draw them apart slowly. Never pull from a single pinch point on a cloud batt; it will tear.

items.problem: The yarn is very uneven with thick and thin spots
items.cause: The pre-drafted strips were not uniform in width before spinning
items.fix: Spend more time on pre-drafting. Each strip should be the same width throughout its length before it reaches the wheel. Uneven pre-draft translates directly into uneven yarn.
